title: The Reporter

jokes.text: See all the spikes? So, two things. One, it's poisonous. And two, it would hurt.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why you can't eat a plant with spikes
jokes.punchline: The redundant obviousness of 'it would hurt' after already saying it's poisonous
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor about stating the obvious, fits Jerry's earnest character well

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't eat a plant with spikes because it's poisonous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It would hurt.

jokes.text: For a while, it was a teenage nature hike, but then we changed it because a girl got pregnant.
jokes.setup: Explaining why they changed from teenage to pre-teen nature hikes
jokes.punchline: The casual delivery of an inappropriate correlation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered matter-of-factly, shows Leslie's oblivious earnestness

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We used to do teenage nature hikes, but we switched to pre-teen because the teenage ones kept getting rained out.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Apparently, once you hit puberty, you attract lightning.

jokes.text: Leslie eating the honeysuckle despite Jerry saying not to eat it
jokes.setup: Jerry explains you don't eat honeysuckle
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately eats it anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Leslie's impulsiveness and disregard for advice

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't eat honeysuckle.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm eating it.

jokes.text: Why did you let me eat that? / I never told you to eat that.
jokes.setup: Leslie ate something she shouldn't have
jokes.punchline: Her blaming Jerry despite him explicitly telling her not to
jokes.explanation: Great character-driven comedy showing Leslie's tendency to deflect blame

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is all your fault, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? I told you not to eat that!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well you should have been more convincing!

jokes.text: Leslie, the animals know you don't eat the country honeysuckle. / I'm not an animal.
jokes.setup: Jerry comparing Leslie unfavorably to animals
jokes.punchline: Leslie's indignant response missing the point
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat showing Leslie's wounded pride

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're like a dog that's been hit by a car. You just keep limping along, hopeful but clearly broken.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Did you just compare me to a dog that's been hit by a car?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I've been hit by a car, Jerry. And I'm still here. Still fighting. Still believing in this town.

jokes.text: She writes for the Pawnee Journal, which is kind of like our town's Washington Post.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing a local paper
jokes.punchline: The absurd comparison to the Washington Post
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's tendency to overstate Pawnee's importance

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The Pawnee Journal is basically the Washington Post of small-town Indiana newspapers. Which means it's nothing like the Washington Post.

jokes.text: The press is a weapon and you can use it to kill people or to feed people.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining press strategy
jokes.punchline: The overwrought metaphor comparing press to weapons
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie over-the-top metaphor delivered with complete sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to treat the press like weapons in an arsenal. Some are sniper rifles - precise, deadly, efficient. Others are howitzers - loud, destructive, but effective at clearing a path. And some are just... regular guns. They shoot bullets.

jokes.text: We had removed five cartoon penises, not even 10%, when we were shut down due to lack of funding.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing a failed project
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact discussion of cartoon penises as a municipal issue
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd government bureaucracy humor delivered with Leslie's earnest professionalism

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So the mural was supposed to be a beautiful representation of our town's history, but apparently the artist interpreted 'founding fathers' very literally, and now we have a giant penis on the side of the Parks Department building.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't see the problem.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, it's a municipal building. We can't have cartoon penises as our public art.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I think it really brings the space together.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I've seen worse things in the parks.

jokes.text: To this day, I am haunted by those remaining penises. One penis in particular.
jokes.setup: Leslie being haunted by the failed graffiti removal
jokes.punchline: The escalation to being haunted by 'one penis in particular'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that takes an already funny premise to an even more absurd level. The specificity of 'one penis in particular' is brilliant character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm still being haunted by that graffiti removal failure. Every night I see it in my dreams.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But now it's worse. I'm being haunted by one penis in particular.

jokes.text: What? Stay on message again? / Yes, it's that important. It's one and two.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining media rules
jokes.punchline: Her obsessive repetition of the same rule twice
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's anxious over-preparation and tendency to repeat important points

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You can't have more than one styrofoam cup per person per day. And you can't have more than one styrofoam cup per person per day.

jokes.text: Wait. Did I ever get it?
jokes.setup: Ann talking about her experience with government
jokes.punchline: Realizing mid-sentence she never got her recycling bin
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing government inefficiency through personal experience

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I've learned so much working in government, and I feel like I've really made a difference in this community. I've helped with so many initiatives and programs, and I'm really proud of the work we've—wait, did I ever get my recycling bin?

jokes.text: This is JJ's Diner, the unofficial meeting place of Pawnee's political elite. The people who eat here basically run this town.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing a diner
jokes.punchline: The visual disconnect between 'political elite' and what's presumably a modest diner
jokes.explanation: Classic small-town grandiosity from Leslie, visual humor in the setting

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This diner is where all the political elite gather to discuss matters of great importance.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It has plastic booths, a counter with spinning stools, and a handwritten menu taped to the window.

jokes.text: What about that thing you did for the middle school newspaper last year? / Oh. No. I don't count that. That was a smear job.
jokes.setup: Mark asking about Leslie's media experience
jokes.punchline: Leslie dismissing a middle school newspaper interview as a 'smear job'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - taking a middle school newspaper way too seriously

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: So you have any media experience?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I was interviewed by the middle school newspaper once.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? What about?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It was a total smear job. They said I was 'overly enthusiastic' and 'didn't understand the concept of recess.'

jokes.text: How you spell your name. / Of course. Because of the silent 'K.'
jokes.setup: Mark predicting the reporter's first question
jokes.punchline: Leslie's confident response about a non-existent silent K
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor - there's no K in Leslie Knope, silent or otherwise. Shows her overthinking everything.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Her first question is gonna be about the silent K in 'knife.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, there is no silent K in 'knife.' The K is completely silent. It's just the word 'nife.' I've checked multiple sources.

jokes.text: How many discussion questions do you have there? / Um... Thirty. / Wow! You're insane.
jokes.setup: Mark asking about Leslie's preparation
jokes.punchline: The reveal of thirty questions and Mark's blunt response
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Leslie's over-preparation and Mark's directness

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: How much did you prepare for this?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I made a list of thirty questions I'm going to ask you.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Of course you did.

jokes.text: Mark and I made love once. And it was very intense.
jokes.setup: Leslie in talking head after discussing strategy with Mark
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable overshare about their past
jokes.explanation: Classic mockumentary overshare moment, fits Leslie's tendency to reveal too much

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Mark and I have been through a lot together. We've seen each other at our worst. I once threw up on his shoes at a Sweetums party, and he never said a word about it. That's friendship.

jokes.text: Who left the door open? No! No! No!
jokes.setup: Leslie giving a tour of the building
jokes.punchline: Someone panicking about a door being left open (presumably letting in raccoons)
jokes.explanation: Weak callback to the raccoon article, mostly just background chaos

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: And this is our storage closet. We keep all our important documents here.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: Oh no! Who left this door open?!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: The raccoons are going to get in! The raccoons are going to get in!

jokes.text: And I agree with you. They are nature's bandits.
jokes.setup: Continuing discussion about raccoons
jokes.punchline: Leslie's earnest agreement with the 'nature's bandits' phrase
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Leslie's tendency to agree enthusiastically

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes! Nature's bandits! That's exactly what they are. They're like little masked criminals, but instead of robbing banks, they're robbing our garbage cans. Which, honestly, is kind of adorable when you think about it.

jokes.text: I am always amazed at his quiet dignity right before he's killed by a cannonball.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing a historical mural
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of violent death in a government building
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about inappropriate historical art in a government building

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This mural depicts the founding of Pawnee. See, there's the river, the native peoples, and then over here is where the settlers brutally murdered them all. It's a beautiful piece of our town's history.

jokes.text: I'm surprised no one's complained about this. / Oh, tons of people have. Yeah, we get letters every day.
jokes.setup: Shauna noting the controversial mural
jokes.punchline: Leslie's matter-of-fact admission they get daily complaints
jokes.explanation: Good irony - they acknowledge it's controversial but do nothing about it

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: This mural is pretty controversial.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, we get complaints about it every single day.

jokes.text: No comment. / About what?
jokes.setup: Ron being introduced to a reporter
jokes.punchline: His preemptive 'no comment' before any question is asked
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson character moment - his reflexive distrust of media and government interaction

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No comment.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: I haven't asked you anything yet.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I work fast.

jokes.text: Hey, Haverford, maybe one day you'll figure out how to spell a three-letter word.
jokes.setup: Ron addressing Tom about Scrabble
jokes.punchline: The insult about not being able to spell three-letter words
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction establishing Ron's dominance and Tom's incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tom, you can't spell a three-letter word. You're an idiot.

jokes.text: I play a lot of online Scrabble with my boss, Ron Swanson, and, oh, my God, that guy is the best. He beats me every time. He kills me. He's awesome. I can't beat him. I should just close my account.
jokes.setup: Tom describing his relationship with Ron
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top enthusiasm about consistently losing
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's trying-too-hard personality and weird relationship with authority

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron and I have this beautiful thing where I'm constantly trying to impress him, and I'm constantly failing. It's like a dance. A dance that I'm losing. Every single time. But I keep dancing.

jokes.text: Tom Haverford, boy genius. Smooth like milk chocolate.
jokes.setup: Leslie introducing Tom
jokes.punchline: Tom's self-description as 'smooth like milk chocolate'
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom trying to sound cool but coming off as awkward

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is Tom Haverford, our department's... entrepreneur.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm smooth like milk chocolate.

jokes.text: That's a weird way to describe me.
characters: Unknown - presumably someone reacting to Tom
jokes.setup: Tom's self-description
jokes.punchline: The understated response to his odd metaphor
jokes.explanation: Simple straight-man response that grounds Tom's weirdness

jokes.text: April Ludgate, 19. Cool enough to be invited anywhere and chooses to be here.
jokes.setup: Leslie introducing April
jokes.punchline: The backhanded compliment about choosing to work in parks department
jokes.explanation: Good character introduction that sets up April's disaffected teenager persona

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is April Ludgate. She's our new parks department secretary, and she chose to work here, which means she has terrible judgment.

jokes.text: Yeah. Good. That way it's verbatim.
jokes.setup: Reporter asking to record
jokes.punchline: Leslie's overly eager agreement with unnecessary explanation
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's nervousness and tendency to over-explain

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: Can I record this?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes! Absolutely. I love being recorded. I have a very recordable face. It photographs well from all angles. My voice is also very record-friendly. It's a warm, mellifluous tone that really translates well to audio formats.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. It's actually called a subcommittee, not a committee, so.
jokes.setup: Reporter calling it a committee
jokes.punchline: Leslie's pedantic correction about subcommittee
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - getting bogged down in bureaucratic details during important interview

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: So you're heading up this new committee?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, it's a subcommittee. There's a very important distinction.

jokes.text: Oh. Yeah. That's actually a great story. I just finished up a gig with my band Three Skin, formerly Four Skin, but our bassist left for personal reasons.
jokes.setup: Andy telling the story of how he fell in the pit
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of the band name 'Four Skin' and its reduction to 'Three Skin'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on 'foreskin' delivered with Andy's oblivious earnestness

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So I was in this band called Four Skin, and we were pretty good. We had like a really solid following in Pawnee. But then our drummer got arrested, so we had to change our name to Three Skin.

jokes.text: And I thought I saw a toaster lying in the pit. And I was like, 'Maybe I should get that.' And I fell in and broke my legs.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining why he went into the pit
jokes.punchline: The illogical desire for a random toaster at the bottom of a pit
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - completely nonsensical but delivered with total sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I went into the pit because there was this illogical desire for a random toaster at the bottom of a pit.

jokes.text: Why would you want a toaster that's lying at the bottom of the pit? / I don't know. I was pretty wasted.
jokes.setup: Shauna asking logical question about the toaster
jokes.punchline: Andy's matter-of-fact admission he was drunk
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that makes the absurd situation even worse with new information

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: Why would you put a toaster in a bathtub?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was drunk.

jokes.text: Wait. You were drunk? / Oh, yeah. Totally. / You knew that. / I didn't know that, no.
jokes.setup: The revelation that Andy was drunk
jokes.punchline: Andy assuming Ann knew when she clearly didn't
jokes.explanation: Good relationship comedy showing communication breakdown between Ann and Andy

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You knew I was drunk?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you knew the whole time?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I didn't know anything!

jokes.text: You should've told me, because we gave you anesthesia at the hospital. / I was probably not thinking 'cause I had two broken femurs and I was blackout drunk.
jokes.setup: Ann revealing the medical danger
jokes.punchline: Andy's casual escalation to 'blackout drunk'
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about dangerous medical situation, escalates the drunkenness reveal

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, you could die. You have a serious medical condition.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ann, I'm not going to die. I'm just going to get blackout drunk.

jokes.text: Like you're perfect. You're on the pill. You drink all the time. You're allowed to do that.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to deflect blame
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate revelation of Ann's birth control use
jokes.explanation: Cringe moment showing Andy's complete lack of filter and social awareness

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Look, I didn't eat the last waffle. Ask Ann, she'll tell you.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, why would I—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because she's on birth control, so she's used to keeping track of things!

jokes.text: Thank you so much for bringing that up in front of a reporter.
jokes.setup: Andy revealing personal information to reporter
jokes.punchline: Ann's sarcastic 'thank you' for the inappropriate disclosure
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic response to an embarrassing situation

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been struggling with my sexuality since I was a kid.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Thanks for sharing that with a reporter, Andy.

jokes.text: Oh, Ann. You're so sweet and innocent and pretty. The press are like sharks, and you guys just dumped a bucket of chum in the water.
jokes.setup: Ann trying to be optimistic about the interview
jokes.punchline: Leslie's dramatic shark metaphor for the press
jokes.explanation: Great Leslie metaphor delivered with perfect dramatic flair, shows her media paranoia

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what? The press is like a shark. It can smell blood in the water from a thousand miles away. And once it does, it circles. And circles. And then it attacks.

jokes.text: And it turns out that Ann is on birth-control pills.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing the problems from the interview
jokes.punchline: Her matter-of-fact inclusion of Ann's birth control as a scandal
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's small-town conservative mindset about what constitutes a scandal

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We have a crisis on our hands. The interview revealed several major problems: the pothole on Ramsett Street, the crumbling infrastructure at City Hall, and Ann's birth control.

jokes.text: Do you think I'm in the top five best-looking Indian guys in Pawnee?
jokes.setup: Tom asking April a random question
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific and self-absorbed question
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom vanity moment, the specificity of 'top five' and 'in Pawnee' is funny

jokes.text: No. / Who do you think's got me beat? That guy Hashish at City Planning?
jokes.setup: Tom asking if he's top five best-looking
jokes.punchline: April's immediate 'No' and Tom's follow-up about specific competition
jokes.explanation: Great character interaction - April's deadpan rejection and Tom's inability to let it go

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Am I in your top five best-looking guys?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Top ten?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, who's ahead of me?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Literally almost everyone.

jokes.text: You're worse than my ex-wife, and she's terrible at Scrabble. And she's a bitch.
jokes.setup: Ron talking about Tom's Scrabble skills
jokes.punchline: The escalating insults ending with calling his ex-wife a bitch
jokes.explanation: Good Ron character moment - blunt, escalating insults delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tom can't play Scrabble. He doesn't have the intellectual capacity. Last week he tried to use the word 'Q' as a standalone tile. When I told him that wasn't allowed, he argued with me for twenty minutes about the rules of a game he's played since childhood. He's an idiot. His mother's an idiot. His father's an idiot. And his ex-wife Wendy is a bitch.

jokes.text: Her name is Tammy Swanson and she's a serious bitch.
jokes.setup: Ron mentioning his ex-wife
jokes.punchline: The emphatic repetition and upgrade to 'serious bitch'
jokes.explanation: Sets up Ron's ex-wife as a recurring character, shows his bitterness

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She's a bitch.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A serious bitch.

jokes.text: She got here 15 minutes late. She's wearing the same dress she wore yesterday. And she had to get a ride from...
jokes.setup: Leslie listing reasons Shauna is unprofessional
jokes.punchline: The petty observations building to the implication about Mark
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's judgmental side and building awareness of Mark/Shauna situation

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: She wears her lanyard on the outside of her shirt. She microwaves fish in the office kitchen. She refers to herself in the third person. She once told me that she doesn't believe in email. And most damning of all, she's dating Mark Brendanawicz.

jokes.text: How big is the lot? / I don't know. You tell me. How big is the lot? / A hundred or something. Give or take 100. I don't know. / A hundred what? / I don't know, Shauna Malwae-Tweep.
jokes.setup: Simple question about lot size
jokes.punchline: Leslie's complete breakdown and nonsensical answers
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment showing Leslie's complete meltdown under pressure

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: What's the lot size?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I don't know, maybe like... a lot? A regular lot? It's got the lot things on it, you know, dirt and trees and... molecules.

jokes.text: I just played 'lexicons' for a billion points. / No, no, no, no, no. What? I was letting him win, dumbass.
jokes.setup: Tom bragging about his Scrabble move
jokes.punchline: April revealing she was letting Ron win and Tom messed it up
jokes.explanation: Good plot twist that recontextualizes the Scrabble dynamic, shows April's secret competence

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I just played 'qi' on a triple word score. That's 47 points. I am dominating this game.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, about that. Ron and I have a thing where I let him win at games so he doesn't get angry and fire us.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You just ruined six months of work.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, what?

jokes.text: Crap on a stick. Okay, technically, yes, it was nepotism. But... Oh, my God. No, it wasn't.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizing she admitted to nepotism
jokes.punchline: Her immediate contradiction of herself
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Leslie realizing she's making things worse

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I would never hire someone just because they're family. That would be nepotism, and nepotism is wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Also, my sister starts Monday.

jokes.text: There was one annoying thing. She and Mark had sex with each other. / You're kidding. / I wish I was, but I'm not. They did it.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing the interview as going well
jokes.punchline: Her casual revelation about Mark and Shauna having sex
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's priorities - treating the sex as just an 'annoying thing'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The interview went really well. I think we really connected. She asked me about my vision for the Parks Department, and I talked about some of the initiatives I want to implement.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, and apparently Mark and Shauna have been having sex. I don't know when that started, but good for them, I guess.

jokes.text: Called it. I called that. / You remember that? Yeah. / He did, actually. He totally called that.
jokes.setup: Revelation about Mark and Shauna
jokes.punchline: Andy's pride in predicting it and the others confirming
jokes.explanation: Andy getting credit for obvious prediction, shows his occasional unexpected insights

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I called it! I knew Mark and Shauna were gonna hook up!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You did call that.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yeah, you totally did.

jokes.text: He was probably thinking with the head of his wiener instead of the head of his brain.
jokes.setup: Discussion about why men behave badly
jokes.punchline: Andy's crude but earnest anatomical metaphor
jokes.explanation: Crude but fits Andy's childish way of expressing adult concepts

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's like, we got a head and a butt, and just enough blood to run one of them at a time.

jokes.text: I bet he didn't use a condom. / Andy, God!
jokes.setup: Discussion about Mark and Shauna
jokes.punchline: Andy's inappropriate speculation about safe sex
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's complete lack of filter and social awareness

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So Mark and Shauna, huh? I wonder if they use protection.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, that's not really something you should be wondering about.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, Mark seems like the type of guy who'd be into like, helmets and knee pads and stuff. You know, for safety.

jokes.text: But you're being a huge dork.
jokes.setup: Mark warning Leslie about taking something the wrong way
jokes.punchline: His blunt assessment of her behavior as dorky
jokes.explanation: Mark's characteristic bluntness cutting through Leslie's drama

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Leslie, I'm just saying, don't take this the wrong way.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: But you're being kind of dorky right now.

jokes.text: I accidentally ate an old burrito.
jokes.setup: Leslie calling to explain her strange behavior
jokes.punchline: The random excuse of an old burrito causing food poisoning
jokes.explanation: Absurd excuse that shows Leslie's panic and inability to think clearly

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hi, yes, I know I've been acting strange all day. I found an old burrito in the back of my desk drawer and I think it gave me food poisoning.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, I don't know how long it's been there. No, I'm not going to a doctor. I'm going to ride this out with ginger ale and determination.

jokes.text: I'm assuming not Mexican. / Why? / Because of the burrito. / Oh! Yeah, well, it wasn't a Mexican burrito.
jokes.setup: Leslie suggesting lunch after claiming food poisoning from burrito
jokes.punchline: Her nonsensical distinction about it not being a Mexican burrito
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie digging herself deeper with illogical explanations

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm not saying I have food poisoning. I'm saying that burrito was not a Mexican burrito.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It means it was a burrito made by someone who hates Mexican people.

jokes.text: Can I say over 100%? Because I would be lying if I said less.
jokes.setup: Asked about odds the park will get made
jokes.punchline: Her mathematically impossible confidence level
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie optimism taken to absurd mathematical extremes

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The odds of this park getting made? I'd say... 100%.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's not how odds work.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're right. 150%.

jokes.text: Series of increasingly ridiculous quotes about the park never happening, including unicorns, Pope getting married, talking monkeys, leprechauns, and the sun falling out of the sky
jokes.setup: Reading negative quotes about the park project
jokes.punchline: The escalating absurdity of the comparisons people made
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating structure of increasingly absurd analogies, great writing that builds momentum

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: One person said the park is 'worse than a dumpster fire.' Another said it's 'worse than that time a sinkhole swallowed a bus.' Someone compared it to 'the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs.' And then this one person just wrote, 'worse than my marriage.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, at least the asteroid part was factually accurate.

jokes.text: Oh, really? Tell that to a 14-year-old girl. / There are some countries where the Pope can be married. / Really? Have you seen The Wizard of Oz? / Leprechauns exist.
jokes.setup: Leslie responding to the ridiculous analogies
jokes.punchline: Her increasingly desperate and illogical counterarguments
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy showing Leslie's refusal to accept any negative comparison, no matter how absurd

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That doesn't even make sense! A blanket is nothing like a government!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And another thing - you can't just compare taxes to ice cream cones!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how logic works! A pothole is not a metaphor for democracy!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, you know what? Fine. A sandwich is exactly like city zoning regulations. You happy now?

jokes.text: Mark Brendanawicz.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking who said the park would never happen
jokes.punchline: The revelation it was Mark, Leslie's committee member
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic beat - the betrayal reveal delivered with maximum impact

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Who said the park would never happen?

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: Mark did.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Mark? Mark Brendanawicz?

jokes.text: Do you have to say it's off the record? / Oh, God.
jokes.setup: Mark claiming his quotes were off the record
jokes.punchline: His realization he doesn't understand how journalism works
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Mark's ignorance about media protocols

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Those quotes were off the record.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Mark, that's not how journalism works. Once you say something to a reporter, it's on the record.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Well, nobody told me that.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's like... basic journalism 101.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I went to journalism school for a week.

jokes.text: Come on, stay in bed. That park is never, ever, ever gonna get made. / Right. And then I came back to bed. / Yeah, but the important part of what I said was, 'Stay in bed.'
jokes.setup: Mark explaining context of his quotes
jokes.punchline: His attempt to reframe devastating quotes as romantic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Mark's desperation and misplaced priorities

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I said, 'I'm not interested in talking to you about your problems.' She thought that was romantic because I was being honest.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: And when I told her, 'I don't think you're very smart,' she said it was the most vulnerable thing anyone had ever said to her.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Even when I said, 'You're kind of a disappointment,' she took it as a sign that I cared enough to be critical.

jokes.text: I wouldn't say romantically involved. You know, going forward.
jokes.setup: Shauna saying they won't print quotes since they're romantically involved
jokes.punchline: Mark immediately trying to walk back the relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing Mark's callousness and poor timing

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: We're not romantically involved. We barely know each other. I mean, I know her name is Shauna, but that's about it. We've never even been on a date.

jokes.text: I don't even know what lexicons are. I thought that was a luxury automobile.
jokes.setup: Tom defending himself about the Scrabble word
jokes.punchline: His confusion of 'lexicons' with 'Lexus'
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay showing Tom's materialism and ignorance

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Lexicon is totally a word. It means like, a dictionary or a book of words. It's from Latin.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, no. Lexicon is a Lexus. A Lexus is a fancy car. So lexicon is a fancy book of words.

jokes.text: You can't even spell vocabulary. / Yeah. V-O-G-X... Was that right? No.
jokes.setup: Ron insulting Tom's spelling
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate demonstration of the point by misspelling 'vocabulary'
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff structure where Tom immediately proves Ron's point

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Your vocabulary is abysmal.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, my vocabullairy is just fine, thank you very much.

jokes.text: Tom is exactly what I'm looking for in a government employee.
jokes.setup: Ron listing Tom's negative qualities
jokes.punchline: His conclusion that these make Tom perfect for government work
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment that perfectly encapsulates Ron's libertarian philosophy and disdain for government efficiency

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tom is lazy, incompetent, and has no integrity whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Which makes him the perfect government employee.

jokes.text: Whoa! You nailed Malwae-Tweep? Nice. / Yes, but I'm not gonna do it again. It's over. / So she's available.
jokes.setup: Mark admitting he slept with the reporter
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate interest when Mark says it's over
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tom's opportunistic and shallow approach to relationships

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: It's over.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, what? You slept with her?

jokes.text: Man, that dude has stuck it in some crazy chicks.
jokes.setup: Mark leaving after being reinstated to the committee
jokes.punchline: Tom's crude assessment of Mark's romantic history
jokes.explanation: Crude Tom moment that fits his character but doesn't add much beyond shock value

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mark's like the human equivalent of a participation trophy. Nobody wanted him, but the system made us keep him around.

jokes.text: 'Pretty drunk, Ann's on the pill.' / I didn't 'throw up,' I spit up.
jokes.setup: Leslie reading the bad parts of the article
jokes.punchline: Ann's petty correction about spitting vs throwing up
jokes.explanation: Shows Ann's concern with minor details while missing the bigger picture

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It says here that you spit on a police officer.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I didn't spit on him, I threw up on him. There's a difference.

jokes.text: She writes, and I quote, 'We'll see.' / Ends on a hopeful note.
jokes.setup: Leslie reading the article's ending
jokes.punchline: Her optimistic interpretation of the noncommittal 'We'll see'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - finding hope in the most minimal possible endorsement, great way to end the episode

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: "We'll see." Oh! That's so encouraging! That means they're definitely going to do it!

tmdb_overview: Leslie arranges for a reporter to do a story about her park project, but she and her committee have the worst time staying on topic. She then calls Mark to help her save the story, but it ends up hurting more than helping. Meanwhile, Tom does all he can to suck up to his boss.
insight: Early-season awkwardness: 66 jokes in 23 minutes, but cringe outweighs laughs consistently.
